claim: WHO reports that dengue incidence has grown dramatically and identifies climate change, high rainfall, humidity, urbanization, and surveillance limits as factors associated with dengue epidemic risk.
method: Reviewed WHO fact sheet lines covering global burden, risk factors, transmission, prevention, and WHO response.
limitations: Global fact sheet, not a district-level Viet Nam dataset.
Associations do not by themselves prove local causal thresholds.

claim: WHO Viet Nam describes dengue in Viet Nam as unstable, seasonally peaking from June to October, with central and southern regions carrying most cases and deaths.

source.title: WHO Viet Nam dengue health topic page

method: Reviewed WHO Viet Nam dengue topic page for country-specific seasonality and regional burden statements.
limitations: Fetched page did not expose a clear last-updated date; source_date is set to access date and should be replaced if WHO metadata becomes available.
Page summarizes historical burden and does not provide current district-level time series.

claim: A peer-reviewed Viet Nam study found that dengue emergence is shaped by interactions among temperature, hydrometeorology, infrastructure, and mobility using 23 years of district-level surveillance data.

source.title: Interactions between climate change, urban infrastructure and mobility are driving dengue emergence in Vietnam

method: Reviewed abstract, methods summary, results, and discussion of the Nature Communications article.
limitations: Peer-reviewed modeling evidence still needs replication before operational use.
Passive surveillance data can reflect reporting access, not only true incidence.
